text: This fifty nine years old female presented with lower abdominal discomfort and off and on blood in the stool. On colonoscopy she was detected to have multiple taberticula limited to the sigmoid colon with signs of inflammation. Plan was laparoscopic sigmoidectomy.

In the left lower quadrant of the abdomen, the surgeon carefully separates adhesions caused by diverticulitis. The small bowel, adherent to both the sigmoid colon and the anterior abdominal wall, is meticulously detached. Thick fibrous adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the abdominal wall are separated, revealing inflamed diverticula and the visible tinea coli of the sigmoid colon, while maintaining hemostasis and avoiding fecal contamination.

The surgeon dissects the right leaf of the mesosigmoid, identifying the sacral promontory. Above the sacral promontory, the mesocolon is divided, revealing the sigmoid branches of the inferior mesenteric artery. The first sigmoid branch is isolated, clipped, and divided using harmonic shears. The remaining two sigmoid branches are similarly identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon continues the dissection downwards to the rectosigmoid junction, exposing the posterior wall of the rectosigmoid and ensuring all diverticula are included. The peritoneal incision is then turned anteriorly and to the left to divide the mesocolon. The muscular fibers of the anterior surface of the rectosigmoid are visible, along with the pulsations of the preserved inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection does not go very deep posteriorly, focusing on the left lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the rectosigmoid junction and carries the incision upwards along the left paracolic gutter, mobilizing the lower portion of the left colon. The dissection proceeds downwards, identifying the gonadal vessels and ensuring the left ureter is safeguarded, with visible uretic peristalsis.

The surgeon continues the incision on the left side of the rectosigmoid towards the pelvis, joining the previous incision from the right side. The posterior surface of the rectosigmoid is fully mobilized and free. After circumferential dissection and removal of fat, an echelon-sixty stapler with a green load is used to divide the rectosigmoid. The colon is exteriorized through an incision in the left iliac fossa, and the specimen is excised. The anvil of the stapler is placed, a purse string is applied, and the colon is pushed inward.

A 33 French SDH stapler is introduced trans-anally, with the spike advancing through the staple line while supporting the rectal wall. The anvil of the stapler is locked into place at the sigmoid colon, and the ends of the sigmoid and rectum are approximated by closing the stapler, ensuring no fat or intestine is trapped between them. The stapler is activated to complete the anastomosis, and then gradually removed. The anastomosis is tested by injecting saline from below to check for integrity.
